Use structured exception handling (Try...Catch...Finally) exclusively; never use On Error GoTo or On Error Resume Next.
Catch the most specific exception type first, then more general types (e.g., Catch ex As FileNotFoundException before Catch ex As IOException).
Always include a Finally block or Using statement for resource cleanup (database connections, file handles, streams).
Use the Using statement (Using conn As New SqlConnection(...)) for all IDisposable objects to guarantee cleanup.
Log exceptions with full details (message, stack trace, inner exceptions) using a logging framework (Serilog, NLog, or Microsoft.Extensions.Logging).
Throw meaningful custom exceptions (inheriting from Exception) for domain-specific error conditions.
Use Throw (without an argument) to re-throw exceptions and preserve the original stack trace instead of Throw ex.
Validate inputs early with guard clauses and throw ArgumentNullException, ArgumentException, or ArgumentOutOfRangeException before processing begins.
Return consistent error responses in MVC controllers (e.g., appropriate HTTP status codes) and surface user-friendly messages in MVVM view models.
Use global exception handling middleware (in ASP.NET MVC) or Application.UnhandledException / AppDomain.UnhandledException for last-resort error capture.
Use ExceptionFilterAttribute or middleware in ASP.NET MVC to centralize cross-cutting error handling logic.
Wrap third-party library exceptions in your own exception types at service boundaries to avoid leaking implementation details.
